---
title: "Fundermentals of R"
subtitle: "Advanced R - 2.0 Data Transformation"
author: "Prepared By Clinton Moshe Mwachia"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output: 
  html_document:
    toc: true
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

## Introduction

Data transformation is a crucial step in data analysis and modeling. In R, there are various techniques available to transform and manipulate data. In this lesson, we will explore several important data transformation operations, including normalization, standardization, data aggregation, transforming data types, mutating columns and rows, merging data frames, melting data frames, casting data frames, and reshaping data.

## Normalization

Normalization is the process of scaling numeric data to a specific range, typically between 0 and 1, to ensure that all variables have equal importance. There are different methods available for normalization, including Min-Max normalization, Z-Score normalization, and Decimal scaling.

### Min-Max Normalization

```{r min-max-norm}
# Sample data
data <- c(5, 10, 15, 20, 25)

# Min-Max normalization
normalized_data <- (data - min(data)) / (max(data) - min(data))
normalized_data
```

### Z-score Normalization

```{r z-score-norm}

```

### Decimal Normalization

```{r dec-norm}

```

## Standardization

Standardization is the process of transforming numeric data to have a mean of 0 and a standard deviation of 1. The most common method for standardization is Z-Score standardization.

### Z-score standardization

```{r z-score-std}
standardized_data <- (data - mean(data)) / sd(data)

standardized_data
```

*Tip: Confirm the mean and sd of both data and standardized_data, compare them*

## Data Aggregation

Data aggregation involves combining multiple rows into a single row based on specific grouping variables. This is useful for summarizing data or creating aggregated statistics.

### Method 1

```{r data-agg-1}
# Sample data
df <- data.frame(Category = c("A", "B", "A", "B"),
                 Value = c(10, 15, 5, 8))
df
# sum data by category
aggregate(Value ~ Category, data = df, FUN = sum)

# sum data by category
aggregate(Value ~ Category, data = df, FUN = mean)
```

### Method 2

```{r data-agg-2}
# loading the required libraries
library(dplyr) # provides group_by
library(magrittr) # provide %>% operator

# sum data by category
df %>%
  group_by(Category) %>%
  summarise(total = sum(Value))


# sum data by category
df %>%
  group_by(Category) %>%
  summarise(Avg = mean(Value))
```

## Transforming Data Types

R provides functions to convert data from one type to another. This is useful when working with data that needs to be in a specific format, such as converting character data to numeric or vice versa.

```{r df-dataType}
# check the typeof category
class(df$Category)
# converting category from character to factor
df$Category = as.factor(df$Category) 

# confirm new class
class(df$Category)
```

## Mutating Columns

Mutating columns involves modifying existing columns or adding new columns based on specific operations or conditions. Lets add a new column called `Test` to our data frame.

### Method 1

```{r mutate-col-1}
# adding column Test to the df
df$Test = c(100, 200, 300, 400)

# display df
df
```

### Method 2

```{r mutate-col-2}
# adding column Test to the df
df = df %>%
  mutate(Test2 = c(102, 202, 302, 402))

# display df
df
```

### Bonus

You can also Mutate based on an existing column

```{r mutate-col-bonus}
# adding column Test3 which contains value multiplied by 2
df$Test3 = df$Value * 2

df
```

## Adding Rows

```{r bind-rows}
# create a new df with the rows
new_df = data.frame(
  Category=c("C"),
  Value=c(9),
  Test=c(9),
  Test2=c(9),
  Test3=c(9)
)

# adding the row
df = rbind(df, new_df)

df
```

## Data Frames

### Merging Data Frames

Merging data frames allows you to combine data from multiple data frames based on common variables. This is useful when you need to consolidate data from different sources or datasets.

```{r merg-df}
# Sample data frames
df1 <- data.frame(ID = c(1, 2, 3),
                  Name = c("John", "Emily", "David"))

df2 <- data.frame(ID = c(2, 3, 4),
                  Age = c(25, 30, 35))

# Merging data frames based on ID
merged_df <- merge(df1, df2, by = "ID")

merged_df
```

*POINT TO THINK ABOUT: What if there is no common column?*

### Melting Data Frames

Melting a data frame reshapes it from a wide format to a long format, where each row represents a unique observation. This is useful when working with data that has multiple variables or columns.

```{r melt-df}
# loading library
library(reshape2)

# melting the df1
melted_df = melt(df1, value.name = "ID")
```

**WE WILL USE THIS TECHNIQUE IN THE NEXT LESSON**

### Casting Data Frames

Casting a data frame reshapes it from a long format to a wide format, where each row represents a unique observation. This is useful when you want to pivot or summarize data based on specific variables.

```{r cast-df}
dcast(melted_df, Name~variable)
```

### Reshaping Data

Reshaping data involves restructuring data from one format to another, such as converting data from long to wide or wide to long format. The reshape2 package in R provides functions for reshaping data frames.

```{r reshape-df}
# Sample data frame
df3 <- data.frame(ID = c(1, 2),
                 A_2019 = c(10, 20),
                 B_2019 = c(15, 25),
                 A_2020 = c(30, 40),
                 B_2020 = c(35, 45))

# Reshaping the data frame from wide to long format
melt(df3, id.vars = "ID", variable.name = "Year", value.name = "Value")

```

HURRAY! YOU HAVE FINISHED THE LESSON, PROCEED TO THE ASSIGNMENT.
